---
title: "About"
body-classes: about-page text-page
---

::: {.page-intro}
I could give you the polished version. This is the more accurate one.
:::

::: {.page-shell}

The official bio and the resume are both true. They are also compressed. This page is for the patterns that show up more reliably than either of those documents.

::: {.word-threadline}
This page moves through six threads:
[Salvage](#salvage),
[Tinkering](#tinkering),
[Inquiry](#inquiry),
[Signals](#signals),
[Stakes](#stakes),
and [In Plain Terms](#plain-terms).
:::

::: {.word-section}
## Salvage {#salvage}

I am drawn to things that are broken but recoverable. That includes objects, workflows, explanations, websites, and any system that is almost useful but not quite there yet. Repair feels less like a hobby to me than a way of thinking.

```{=html}
<details class="trace-log">
  <summary>Recent traces</summary>
  <ul class="trace-list">
    <li><span class="trace-date">March 2026</span><span class="trace-entry">Rebuilt this site because it felt too dressed up and not precise enough.</span></li>
    <li><span class="trace-date">March 2026</span><span class="trace-entry">Debugged a stubborn render pipeline instead of pretending the weird output was acceptable.</span></li>
    <li><span class="trace-date">Ongoing</span><span class="trace-entry">Keep a mental inventory of objects that look one careful afternoon away from becoming useful again.</span></li>
  </ul>
</details>
```
:::

::: {.word-section}
## Tinkering {#tinkering}

I like making things in a provisional, iterative way. Dashboards, interfaces, figures, poems, and small web experiments all belong here. Tinkering is what happens when curiosity is allowed to touch the materials directly instead of staying theoretical.

```{=html}
<details class="trace-log">
  <summary>Recent traces</summary>
  <ul class="trace-list">
    <li><span class="trace-date">March 2026</span><span class="trace-entry">Turned the poetry page into a quieter reading space with foldouts, marginalia, and favorite-line hearts.</span></li>
    <li><span class="trace-date">March 2026</span><span class="trace-entry">Kept simplifying page layouts until the writing had more room than the styling.</span></li>
    <li><span class="trace-date">Ongoing</span><span class="trace-entry">Use design as a way to make complicated work legible without sanding off what makes it interesting.</span></li>
  </ul>
</details>
```
:::

::: {.word-section}
## Inquiry {#inquiry}

Most of my learning happens by following a question until it becomes inconvenient. Remote sensing, deep learning, web scraping, environmental data systems, and whatever tool or method makes the next layer of the problem visible all tend to accumulate this way.

```{=html}
<details class="trace-log">
  <summary>Recent traces</summary>
  <ul class="trace-list">
    <li><span class="trace-date">2025-2026</span><span class="trace-entry">Spent a great deal of time with CNNs, U-Net segmentation, and irrigation mapping.</span></li>
    <li><span class="trace-date">2026</span><span class="trace-entry">Keep moving between cloud geospatial infrastructure, reproducible pipelines, and the questions that make them worth building.</span></li>
    <li><span class="trace-date">Habit</span><span class="trace-entry">Read widely, underline aggressively, and keep too many tabs open until a pattern emerges.</span></li>
  </ul>
</details>
```
:::

::: {.word-section}
## Signals {#signals}

I pay attention to the small detail that gives away the larger system. Sometimes that is a strange edge pixel, a mislabeled figure, or a workflow assumption that is quietly steering everything in the wrong direction. Sometimes it is a tide line, a cleanup bucket, or the way a place makes its condition obvious if you look long enough.

```{=html}
<details class="trace-log">
  <summary>Recent traces</summary>
  <ul class="trace-list">
    <li><span class="trace-date">Always</span><span class="trace-entry">Trust the odd detail enough to investigate it before dismissing it as noise.</span></li>
    <li><span class="trace-date">Field habit</span><span class="trace-entry">Notice what kinds of care a place has or has not been receiving.</span></li>
    <li><span class="trace-date">Working habit</span><span class="trace-entry">Prefer the clue that explains the whole mess to the summary that hides it.</span></li>
  </ul>
</details>
```
:::

::: {.word-section}
## Stakes {#stakes}

I care most about work that is useful, rigorous, and materially good for the world it touches. Conservation, restoration, stewardship, good infrastructure, and clear communication all sit in the same category for me. I am not very interested in technical theater. I am interested in work that matters.

```{=html}
<details class="trace-log">
  <summary>Recent traces</summary>
  <ul class="trace-list">
    <li><span class="trace-date">Present</span><span class="trace-entry">Working on environmental data tools that make large, complicated datasets easier to use without flattening them.</span></li>
    <li><span class="trace-date">Recent years</span><span class="trace-entry">Keep showing up for beach cleanups because care works better as a practice than an identity.</span></li>
    <li><span class="trace-date">General rule</span><span class="trace-entry">If a project is technically clever but not useful to anyone, I lose interest quickly.</span></li>
  </ul>
</details>
```
:::

::: {.word-section .word-section-final}
## In Plain Terms {#plain-terms}

I am an environmental data scientist, builder, and creative person with a compulsive fixing instinct. Most of what I do lives somewhere between remote sensing, machine learning, environmental data infrastructure, design, and communication.

What that looks like in practice is research, code, reproducible workflows, interfaces, poetry, photography, stewardship, and learning whatever the problem requires next.

```{=html}
<p class="word-links">
  <a href="research.html">Research</a>
  <a href="cv.html">Resume</a>
  <a href="art.html">Art &amp; Poetry</a>
  <a href="mailto:ermiller@ucsb.edu">Email</a>
</p>
```
:::

:::
